your_job_title_and_major_duties: Data Analyst – Responsible for collecting, cleaning, and analyzing large datasets for mid-sized healthcare and retail clients. Built automated reports and interactive dashboards in Tableau and Power BI to track KPIs such as patient readmission rates, appointment no-show rates, inventory turnover, and customer retention. Wrote SQL queries to extract and join data from multiple relational databases (primarily PostgreSQL and SQL Server) and used Python (pandas, NumPy) for data preprocessing, exploratory analysis, and basic predictive modeling (e.g., churn risk scoring, demand forecasting). Collaborated closely with project managers and client stakeholders to translate ambiguous business questions into specific analytical approaches, presented findings in non-technical language, and created detailed documentation so that internal teams could reproduce analyses. Also helped mentor two junior analysts by reviewing their queries and code, walking them through debugging common data-quality issues, and co-leading a monthly internal "best practices in analytics" lunch-and-learn series.
reason_for_leaving: I am relocating from San Francisco, CA to Portland, OR to be closer to my partner and extended family, and my current employer does not offer a fully remote option for my role on a permanent basis. Over the past year, I have been splitting my time between the Bay Area and the Pacific Northwest, which has become increasingly difficult to manage logistically and personally. After multiple conversations with my supervisor about potential internal transfers or a remote arrangement, we mutually agreed that the best path forward would be for me to transition out of my position once my current client projects were successfully handed off. I provided over two months’ notice, documented my ongoing analyses and reporting workflows in detail, trained a junior analyst to take over my main accounts, and stayed on through the completion of a major year-end reporting cycle so that the transition would be as smooth as possible for the team and for our clients. My decision to leave is not related to performance, dissatisfaction with the work, or any disciplinary issues; it is driven primarily by family considerations and a desire to find a new role in the Portland area where I can continue to grow in analytics while having a more stable personal routine.
